lines: Verona, for a while I take my leave
To see my friends in Padua, but of all
My best beloved and approved friend,
Hortensio. And I trow this is his house.
Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.
fullText: Verona, for a while I take my leave To see my friends in Padua, but of all My best beloved and approved friend, Hortensio. And I trow this is his house. Here, sirrah Grumio, knock, I say.

lines: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.
fullText: Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.

lines: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate
And rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's pate.
fullText: Villain, I say, knock me at this gate And rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's pate.

lines: Will it not be?
Faith, sirrah, an you'll not knock, I'll ring it.
I'll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.
fullText: Will it not be? Faith, sirrah, an you'll not knock, I'll ring it. I'll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.

lines: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah
villain.
fullText: Now knock when I bid you, sirrah villain.

lines: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray?
Con tutto il cuore ben trovato, may I say.
fullText: Signior Hortensio, come you to part the fray? Con tutto il cuore ben trovato, may I say.

lines: A senseless villain, good Hortensio.
I bade the rascal knock upon your gate
And could not get him for my heart to do it.
fullText: A senseless villain, good Hortensio. I bade the rascal knock upon your gate And could not get him for my heart to do it.

lines: Sirrah, begone, or talk not, I advise you.
fullText: Sirrah, begone, or talk not, I advise you.

lines: Such wind as scatters young men through the world
To seek their fortunes farther than at home,
Where small experience grows. But in a few,
Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me:
Antonio, my father, is deceased,
And I have thrust myself into this maze,
Happily to wive and thrive, as best I may.
Crowns in my purse I have and goods at home,
And so am come abroad to see the world.
fullText: Such wind as scatters young men through the world To seek their fortunes farther than at home, Where small experience grows. But in a few, Signior Hortensio, thus it stands with me: Antonio, my father, is deceased, And I have thrust myself into this maze, Happily to wive and thrive, as best I may. Crowns in my purse I have and goods at home, And so am come abroad to see the world.

lines: Signior Hortensio, 'twixt such friends as we
Few words suffice. And therefore, if thou know
One rich enough to be Petruchio's wife
(As wealth is burden of my wooing dance),
Be she as foul as was Florentius' love,
As old as Sibyl, and as curst and shrewd
As Socrates' Xanthippe, or a worse,
She moves me not, or not removes at least
Affection's edge in me, were she as rough
As are the swelling Adriatic seas.
I come to wive it wealthily in Padua;
If wealthily, then happily in Padua.
fullText: Signior Hortensio, 'twixt such friends as we Few words suffice. And therefore, if thou know One rich enough to be Petruchio's wife (As wealth is burden of my wooing dance), Be she as foul as was Florentius' love, As old as Sibyl, and as curst and shrewd As Socrates' Xanthippe, or a worse, She moves me not, or not removes at least Affection's edge in me, were she as rough As are the swelling Adriatic seas. I come to wive it wealthily in Padua; If wealthily, then happily in Padua.

lines: Hortensio, peace. Thou know'st not gold's effect.
Tell me her father's name, and 'tis enough;
For I will board her, though she chide as loud
As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.
fullText: Hortensio, peace. Thou know'st not gold's effect. Tell me her father's name, and 'tis enough; For I will board her, though she chide as loud As thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.

lines: I know her father, though I know not her,
And he knew my deceased father well.
I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her,
And therefore let me be thus bold with you
To give you over at this first encounter--
Unless you will accompany me thither.
fullText: I know her father, though I know not her, And he knew my deceased father well. I will not sleep, Hortensio, till I see her, And therefore let me be thus bold with you To give you over at this first encounter-- Unless you will accompany me thither.

lines: Peace, sirrah.
fullText: Peace, sirrah.

lines: I know she is an irksome, brawling scold.
If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.
fullText: I know she is an irksome, brawling scold. If that be all, masters, I hear no harm.

lines: Born in Verona, old Antonio's son.
My father dead, my fortune lives for me,
And I do hope good days and long to see.
fullText: Born in Verona, old Antonio's son. My father dead, my fortune lives for me, And I do hope good days and long to see.

lines: Will I live?
fullText: Will I live?

lines: Why came I hither but to that intent?
Think you a little din can daunt mine ears?
Have I not in my time heard lions roar?
Have I not heard the sea, puffed up with winds,
Rage like an angry boar chafed with sweat?
Have I not heard great ordnance in the field
And heaven's artillery thunder in the skies?
Have I not in a pitched battle heard
Loud 'larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets clang?
And do you tell me of a woman's tongue,
That gives not half so great a blow to hear
As will a chestnut in a farmer's fire?
Tush, tush, fear boys with bugs!
fullText: Why came I hither but to that intent? Think you a little din can daunt mine ears? Have I not in my time heard lions roar? Have I not heard the sea, puffed up with winds, Rage like an angry boar chafed with sweat? Have I not heard great ordnance in the field And heaven's artillery thunder in the skies? Have I not in a pitched battle heard Loud 'larums, neighing steeds, and trumpets clang? And do you tell me of a woman's tongue, That gives not half so great a blow to hear As will a chestnut in a farmer's fire? Tush, tush, fear boys with bugs!

lines: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.
fullText: Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.

lines: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?
fullText: Hortensio, to what end are all these words?

lines: Sir, sir, the first's for me; let her go by.
fullText: Sir, sir, the first's for me; let her go by.

lines: Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth:
The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for,
Her father keeps from all access of suitors
And will not promise her to any man
Until the elder sister first be wed.
The younger then is free, and not before.
fullText: Sir, understand you this of me, in sooth: The youngest daughter, whom you hearken for, Her father keeps from all access of suitors And will not promise her to any man Until the elder sister first be wed. The younger then is free, and not before.

lines: And you, good sir. Pray, have you not a daughter
Called Katherina, fair and virtuous?
fullText: And you, good sir. Pray, have you not a daughter Called Katherina, fair and virtuous?

lines: You wrong me, Signior Gremio. Give me leave.--
I am a gentleman of Verona, sir,
That hearing of her beauty and her wit,
Her affability and bashful modesty,
Her wondrous qualities and mild behavior,
Am bold to show myself a forward guest
Within your house, to make mine eye the witness
Of that report which I so oft have heard,
And, for an entrance to my entertainment,
I do present you with a man of mine,
Cunning in music and the mathematics,
To instruct her fully in those sciences,
Whereof I know she is not ignorant.
Accept of him, or else you do me wrong.
His name is Litio, born in Mantua.
fullText: You wrong me, Signior Gremio. Give me leave.-- I am a gentleman of Verona, sir, That hearing of her beauty and her wit, Her affability and bashful modesty, Her wondrous qualities and mild behavior, Am bold to show myself a forward guest Within your house, to make mine eye the witness Of that report which I so oft have heard, And, for an entrance to my entertainment, I do present you with a man of mine, Cunning in music and the mathematics, To instruct her fully in those sciences, Whereof I know she is not ignorant. Accept of him, or else you do me wrong. His name is Litio, born in Mantua.

lines: I see you do not mean to part with her,
Or else you like not of my company.
fullText: I see you do not mean to part with her, Or else you like not of my company.

lines: Petruchio is my name, Antonio's son,
A man well known throughout all Italy.
fullText: Petruchio is my name, Antonio's son, A man well known throughout all Italy.

lines: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio, I would fain be
doing.
fullText: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio, I would fain be doing.

lines: Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste,
And every day I cannot come to woo.
You knew my father well, and in him me,
Left solely heir to all his lands and goods,
Which I have bettered rather than decreased.
Then tell me, if I get your daughter's love,
What dowry shall I have with her to wife?
fullText: Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste, And every day I cannot come to woo. You knew my father well, and in him me, Left solely heir to all his lands and goods, Which I have bettered rather than decreased. Then tell me, if I get your daughter's love, What dowry shall I have with her to wife?

lines: And, for that dowry, I'll assure her of
Her widowhood, be it that she survive me,
In all my lands and leases whatsoever.
Let specialties be therefore drawn between us,
That covenants may be kept on either hand.
fullText: And, for that dowry, I'll assure her of Her widowhood, be it that she survive me, In all my lands and leases whatsoever. Let specialties be therefore drawn between us, That covenants may be kept on either hand.

lines: Why, that is nothing. For I tell you, father,
I am as peremptory as she proud-minded;
And where two raging fires meet together,
They do consume the thing that feeds their fury.
Though little fire grows great with little wind,
Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all.
So I to her and so she yields to me,
For I am rough and woo not like a babe.
fullText: Why, that is nothing. For I tell you, father, I am as peremptory as she proud-minded; And where two raging fires meet together, They do consume the thing that feeds their fury. Though little fire grows great with little wind, Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all. So I to her and so she yields to me, For I am rough and woo not like a babe.

lines: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds,
That shakes not, though they blow perpetually.
fullText: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds, That shakes not, though they blow perpetually.

lines: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench.
I love her ten times more than ere I did.
O, how I long to have some chat with her!
fullText: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench. I love her ten times more than ere I did. O, how I long to have some chat with her!

lines: I pray you do. I'll attend her here--
And woo her with some spirit when she comes!
Say that she rail, why then I'll tell her plain
She sings as sweetly as a nightingale.
Say that she frown, I'll say she looks as clear
As morning roses newly washed with dew.
Say she be mute and will not speak a word,
Then I'll commend her volubility
And say she uttereth piercing eloquence.
If she do bid me pack, I'll give her thanks
As though she bid me stay by her a week.
If she deny to wed, I'll crave the day
When I shall ask the banns, and when be married.
But here she comes--and now, Petruchio, speak.
fullText: I pray you do. I'll attend her here-- And woo her with some spirit when she comes! Say that she rail, why then I'll tell her plain She sings as sweetly as a nightingale. Say that she frown, I'll say she looks as clear As morning roses newly washed with dew. Say she be mute and will not speak a word, Then I'll commend her volubility And say she uttereth piercing eloquence. If she do bid me pack, I'll give her thanks As though she bid me stay by her a week. If she deny to wed, I'll crave the day When I shall ask the banns, and when be married. But here she comes--and now, Petruchio, speak.

lines: You lie, in faith, for you are called plain Kate,
And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst.
But Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom,
Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate
(For dainties are all Kates)--and therefore, Kate,
Take this of me, Kate of my consolation:
Hearing thy mildness praised in every town,
Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded
(Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs),
Myself am moved to woo thee for my wife.
fullText: You lie, in faith, for you are called plain Kate, And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst. But Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom, Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate (For dainties are all Kates)--and therefore, Kate, Take this of me, Kate of my consolation: Hearing thy mildness praised in every town, Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded (Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs), Myself am moved to woo thee for my wife.

lines: Why, what's a movable?
fullText: Why, what's a movable?

lines: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.
fullText: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.

lines: Women are made to bear, and so are you.
fullText: Women are made to bear, and so are you.

lines: Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee,
For knowing thee to be but young and light--
fullText: Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee, For knowing thee to be but young and light--

lines: "Should be"--should buzz!
fullText: "Should be"--should buzz!

lines: O slow-winged turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?
fullText: O slow-winged turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?

lines: Come, come, you wasp! I' faith, you are too angry.
fullText: Come, come, you wasp! I' faith, you are too angry.

lines: My remedy is then to pluck it out.
fullText: My remedy is then to pluck it out.

lines: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting?
In his tail.
fullText: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting? In his tail.

lines: Whose tongue?
fullText: Whose tongue?

lines: What, with my tongue in your tail?
Nay, come again, good Kate. I am a gentleman--
fullText: What, with my tongue in your tail? Nay, come again, good Kate. I am a gentleman--

lines: I swear I'll cuff you if you strike again.
fullText: I swear I'll cuff you if you strike again.

lines: A herald, Kate? O, put me in thy books.
fullText: A herald, Kate? O, put me in thy books.

lines: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.
fullText: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.

lines: Nay, come, Kate, come. You must not look so sour.
fullText: Nay, come, Kate, come. You must not look so sour.

lines: Why, here's no crab, and therefore look not sour.
fullText: Why, here's no crab, and therefore look not sour.

lines: Then show it me.
fullText: Then show it me.

lines: What, you mean my face?
fullText: What, you mean my face?

lines: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.
fullText: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.

lines: 'Tis with cares.
fullText: 'Tis with cares.

lines: Nay, hear you, Kate--in sooth, you 'scape not so.
fullText: Nay, hear you, Kate--in sooth, you 'scape not so.

lines: No, not a whit. I find you passing gentle.
'Twas told me you were rough, and coy, and sullen,
And now I find report a very liar.
For thou art pleasant, gamesome, passing
courteous,
But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers.
Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance,
Nor bite the lip as angry wenches will,
Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk.
But thou with mildness entertain'st thy wooers,
With gentle conference, soft, and affable.
Why does the world report that Kate doth limp?
O sland'rous world! Kate like the hazel twig
Is straight, and slender, and as brown in hue
As hazelnuts, and sweeter than the kernels.
O, let me see thee walk! Thou dost not halt.
fullText: No, not a whit. I find you passing gentle. 'Twas told me you were rough, and coy, and sullen, And now I find report a very liar. For thou art pleasant, gamesome, passing courteous, But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers. Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance, Nor bite the lip as angry wenches will, Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk. But thou with mildness entertain'st thy wooers, With gentle conference, soft, and affable. Why does the world report that Kate doth limp? O sland'rous world! Kate like the hazel twig Is straight, and slender, and as brown in hue As hazelnuts, and sweeter than the kernels. O, let me see thee walk! Thou dost not halt.

lines: Did ever Dian so become a grove
As Kate this chamber with her princely gait?
O, be thou Dian and let her be Kate,
And then let Kate be chaste and Dian sportful.
fullText: Did ever Dian so become a grove As Kate this chamber with her princely gait? O, be thou Dian and let her be Kate, And then let Kate be chaste and Dian sportful.

lines: It is extempore, from my mother wit.
fullText: It is extempore, from my mother wit.

lines: Am I not wise?
fullText: Am I not wise?

lines: Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed.
And therefore, setting all this chat aside,
Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented
That you shall be my wife, your dowry 'greed on,
And, will you, nill you, I will marry you.
Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn,
For by this light, whereby I see thy beauty,
Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well,
Thou must be married to no man but me.
For I am he am born to tame you, Kate,
And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate
Conformable as other household Kates.
fullText: Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed. And therefore, setting all this chat aside, Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented That you shall be my wife, your dowry 'greed on, And, will you, nill you, I will marry you. Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn, For by this light, whereby I see thy beauty, Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well, Thou must be married to no man but me. For I am he am born to tame you, Kate, And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate Conformable as other household Kates.

lines: How but well, sir? How but well?
It were impossible I should speed amiss.
fullText: How but well, sir? How but well? It were impossible I should speed amiss.

lines: Father, 'tis thus: yourself and all the world
That talked of her have talked amiss of her.
If she be curst, it is for policy,
For she's not froward, but modest as the dove;
She is not hot, but temperate as the morn.
For patience she will prove a second Grissel,
And Roman Lucrece for her chastity.
And to conclude, we have 'greed so well together
That upon Sunday is the wedding day.
fullText: Father, 'tis thus: yourself and all the world That talked of her have talked amiss of her. If she be curst, it is for policy, For she's not froward, but modest as the dove; She is not hot, but temperate as the morn. For patience she will prove a second Grissel, And Roman Lucrece for her chastity. And to conclude, we have 'greed so well together That upon Sunday is the wedding day.

lines: Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself.
If she and I be pleased, what's that to you?
'Tis bargained 'twixt us twain, being alone,
That she shall still be curst in company.
I tell you, 'tis incredible to believe
How much she loves me. O, the kindest Kate!
She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss
She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath,
That in a twink she won me to her love.
O, you are novices! 'Tis a world to see
How tame, when men and women are alone,
A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew.--
Give me thy hand, Kate. I will unto Venice
To buy apparel 'gainst the wedding day.--
Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests.
I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.
fullText: Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself. If she and I be pleased, what's that to you? 'Tis bargained 'twixt us twain, being alone, That she shall still be curst in company. I tell you, 'tis incredible to believe How much she loves me. O, the kindest Kate! She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath, That in a twink she won me to her love. O, you are novices! 'Tis a world to see How tame, when men and women are alone, A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew.-- Give me thy hand, Kate. I will unto Venice To buy apparel 'gainst the wedding day.-- Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests. I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.

lines: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu.
I will to Venice. Sunday comes apace.
We will have rings, and things, and fine array,
And kiss me, Kate. We will be married o' Sunday.
through different doors.]
fullText: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu. I will to Venice. Sunday comes apace. We will have rings, and things, and fine array, And kiss me, Kate. We will be married o' Sunday. through different doors.]

lines: Come, where be these gallants? Who's at home?
fullText: Come, where be these gallants? Who's at home?

lines: And yet I come not well.
fullText: And yet I come not well.

lines: Were it better I should rush in thus--
But where is Kate? Where is my lovely bride?
How does my father? Gentles, methinks you frown.
And wherefore gaze this goodly company
As if they saw some wondrous monument,
Some comet or unusual prodigy?
fullText: Were it better I should rush in thus-- But where is Kate? Where is my lovely bride? How does my father? Gentles, methinks you frown. And wherefore gaze this goodly company As if they saw some wondrous monument, Some comet or unusual prodigy?

lines: Tedious it were to tell, and harsh to hear.
Sufficeth I am come to keep my word,
Though in some part enforced to digress,
Which at more leisure I will so excuse
As you shall well be satisfied with all.
But where is Kate? I stay too long from her.
The morning wears. 'Tis time we were at church.
fullText: Tedious it were to tell, and harsh to hear. Sufficeth I am come to keep my word, Though in some part enforced to digress, Which at more leisure I will so excuse As you shall well be satisfied with all. But where is Kate? I stay too long from her. The morning wears. 'Tis time we were at church.

lines: Not I, believe me. Thus I'll visit her.
fullText: Not I, believe me. Thus I'll visit her.

lines: Good sooth, even thus. Therefore, ha' done with
words.
To me she's married, not unto my clothes.
Could I repair what she will wear in me,
As I can change these poor accoutrements,
'Twere well for Kate and better for myself.
But what a fool am I to chat with you
When I should bid good morrow to my bride
And seal the title with a lovely kiss!
fullText: Good sooth, even thus. Therefore, ha' done with words. To me she's married, not unto my clothes. Could I repair what she will wear in me, As I can change these poor accoutrements, 'Twere well for Kate and better for myself. But what a fool am I to chat with you When I should bid good morrow to my bride And seal the title with a lovely kiss!

lines: Gentlemen and friends, I thank you for your pains.
I know you think to dine with me today
And have prepared great store of wedding cheer,
But so it is, my haste doth call me hence,
And therefore here I mean to take my leave.
fullText: Gentlemen and friends, I thank you for your pains. I know you think to dine with me today And have prepared great store of wedding cheer, But so it is, my haste doth call me hence, And therefore here I mean to take my leave.

lines: I must away today, before night come.
Make it no wonder. If you knew my business,
You would entreat me rather go than stay.
And, honest company, I thank you all,
That have beheld me give away myself
To this most patient, sweet, and virtuous wife.
Dine with my father, drink a health to me,
For I must hence, and farewell to you all.
fullText: I must away today, before night come. Make it no wonder. If you knew my business, You would entreat me rather go than stay. And, honest company, I thank you all, That have beheld me give away myself To this most patient, sweet, and virtuous wife. Dine with my father, drink a health to me, For I must hence, and farewell to you all.

lines: It may not be.
fullText: It may not be.

lines: It cannot be.
fullText: It cannot be.

lines: I am content.
fullText: I am content.

lines: I am content you shall entreat me stay,
But yet not stay, entreat me how you can.
fullText: I am content you shall entreat me stay, But yet not stay, entreat me how you can.

lines: Grumio, my horse.
fullText: Grumio, my horse.

lines: O Kate, content thee. Prithee, be not angry.
fullText: O Kate, content thee. Prithee, be not angry.

lines: They shall go forward, Kate, at thy command.--
Obey the bride, you that attend on her.
Go to the feast, revel and domineer,
Carouse full measure to her maidenhead,
Be mad and merry, or go hang yourselves.
But for my bonny Kate, she must with me.
Nay, look not big, nor stamp, nor stare, nor fret;
I will be master of what is mine own.
She is my goods, my chattels; she is my house,
My household stuff, my field, my barn,
My horse, my ox, my ass, my anything.
And here she stands, touch her whoever dare.
I'll bring mine action on the proudest he
That stops my way in Padua.--Grumio,
Draw forth thy weapon. We are beset with thieves.
Rescue thy mistress if thou be a man!--
Fear not, sweet wench, they shall not touch thee,
Kate.
I'll buckler thee against a million.
fullText: They shall go forward, Kate, at thy command.-- Obey the bride, you that attend on her. Go to the feast, revel and domineer, Carouse full measure to her maidenhead, Be mad and merry, or go hang yourselves. But for my bonny Kate, she must with me. Nay, look not big, nor stamp, nor stare, nor fret; I will be master of what is mine own. She is my goods, my chattels; she is my house, My household stuff, my field, my barn, My horse, my ox, my ass, my anything. And here she stands, touch her whoever dare. I'll bring mine action on the proudest he That stops my way in Padua.--Grumio, Draw forth thy weapon. We are beset with thieves. Rescue thy mistress if thou be a man!-- Fear not, sweet wench, they shall not touch thee, Kate. I'll buckler thee against a million.

lines: Where be these knaves? What, no man at door
To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse?
Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Phillip?
fullText: Where be these knaves? What, no man at door To hold my stirrup nor to take my horse? Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Phillip?

lines: "Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir!"
You loggerheaded and unpolished grooms.
What? No attendance? No regard? No duty?
Where is the foolish knave I sent before?
fullText: "Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir!" You loggerheaded and unpolished grooms. What? No attendance? No regard? No duty? Where is the foolish knave I sent before?

lines: You peasant swain, you whoreson malt-horse
drudge!
Did I not bid thee meet me in the park
And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?
fullText: You peasant swain, you whoreson malt-horse drudge! Did I not bid thee meet me in the park And bring along these rascal knaves with thee?

lines: Go, rascals, go, and fetch my supper in!
Where are those--
Sit down, Kate, and welcome.
Soud, soud, soud, soud!
fullText: Go, rascals, go, and fetch my supper in! Where are those-- Sit down, Kate, and welcome. Soud, soud, soud, soud!

lines: A whoreson beetle-headed flap-eared knave!--
Come, Kate, sit down. I know you have a stomach.
Will you give thanks, sweet Kate, or else shall I?--
What's this? Mutton?
fullText: A whoreson beetle-headed flap-eared knave!-- Come, Kate, sit down. I know you have a stomach. Will you give thanks, sweet Kate, or else shall I?-- What's this? Mutton?

lines: Who brought it?
fullText: Who brought it?

lines: 'Tis burnt, and so is all the meat.
What dogs are these? Where is the rascal cook?
How durst you, villains, bring it from the dresser
And serve it thus to me that love it not?
There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all!
You heedless joltheads and unmannered slaves!
What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.
fullText: 'Tis burnt, and so is all the meat. What dogs are these? Where is the rascal cook? How durst you, villains, bring it from the dresser And serve it thus to me that love it not? There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all! You heedless joltheads and unmannered slaves! What, do you grumble? I'll be with you straight.

lines: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away,
And I expressly am forbid to touch it,
For it engenders choler, planteth anger,
And better 'twere that both of us did fast
(Since of ourselves, ourselves are choleric)
Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh.
Be patient. Tomorrow 't shall be mended,
And for this night we'll fast for company.
Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.
fullText: I tell thee, Kate, 'twas burnt and dried away, And I expressly am forbid to touch it, For it engenders choler, planteth anger, And better 'twere that both of us did fast (Since of ourselves, ourselves are choleric) Than feed it with such over-roasted flesh. Be patient. Tomorrow 't shall be mended, And for this night we'll fast for company. Come, I will bring thee to thy bridal chamber.

lines: Thus have I politicly begun my reign,
And 'tis my hope to end successfully.
My falcon now is sharp and passing empty,
And, till she stoop, she must not be full-gorged,
For then she never looks upon her lure.
Another way I have to man my haggard,
To make her come and know her keeper's call.
That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites
That bate and beat and will not be obedient.
She ate no meat today, nor none shall eat.
Last night she slept not, nor tonight she shall not.
As with the meat, some undeserved fault
I'll find about the making of the bed,
And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster,
This way the coverlet, another way the sheets.
Ay, and amid this hurly I intend
That all is done in reverend care of her.
And, in conclusion, she shall watch all night,
And, if she chance to nod, I'll rail and brawl,
And with the clamor keep her still awake.
This is a way to kill a wife with kindness.
And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humor.
He that knows better how to tame a shrew,
Now let him speak; 'tis charity to shew.
fullText: Thus have I politicly begun my reign, And 'tis my hope to end successfully. My falcon now is sharp and passing empty, And, till she stoop, she must not be full-gorged, For then she never looks upon her lure. Another way I have to man my haggard, To make her come and know her keeper's call. That is, to watch her, as we watch these kites That bate and beat and will not be obedient. She ate no meat today, nor none shall eat. Last night she slept not, nor tonight she shall not. As with the meat, some undeserved fault I'll find about the making of the bed, And here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster, This way the coverlet, another way the sheets. Ay, and amid this hurly I intend That all is done in reverend care of her. And, in conclusion, she shall watch all night, And, if she chance to nod, I'll rail and brawl, And with the clamor keep her still awake. This is a way to kill a wife with kindness. And thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humor. He that knows better how to tame a shrew, Now let him speak; 'tis charity to shew.

lines: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?
fullText: How fares my Kate? What, sweeting, all amort?

lines: Pluck up thy spirits. Look cheerfully upon me.
Here, love, thou seest how diligent I am,
To dress thy meat myself and bring it thee.
I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks.
What, not a word? Nay then, thou lov'st it not,
And all my pains is sorted to no proof.
Here, take away this dish.
fullText: Pluck up thy spirits. Look cheerfully upon me. Here, love, thou seest how diligent I am, To dress thy meat myself and bring it thee. I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks. What, not a word? Nay then, thou lov'st it not, And all my pains is sorted to no proof. Here, take away this dish.

lines: The poorest service is repaid with thanks,
And so shall mine before you touch the meat.
fullText: The poorest service is repaid with thanks, And so shall mine before you touch the meat.

lines: Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.--
Much good do it unto thy gentle heart.
Kate, eat apace.
And now, my honey love,
Will we return unto thy father's house
And revel it as bravely as the best,
With silken coats and caps and golden rings,
With ruffs and cuffs and farthingales and things,
With scarves and fans and double change of brav'ry,
With amber bracelets, beads, and all this knav'ry.
What, hast thou dined? The tailor stays thy leisure
To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure.
fullText: Eat it up all, Hortensio, if thou lovest me.-- Much good do it unto thy gentle heart. Kate, eat apace. And now, my honey love, Will we return unto thy father's house And revel it as bravely as the best, With silken coats and caps and golden rings, With ruffs and cuffs and farthingales and things, With scarves and fans and double change of brav'ry, With amber bracelets, beads, and all this knav'ry. What, hast thou dined? The tailor stays thy leisure To deck thy body with his ruffling treasure.

lines: Why, this was molded on a porringer!
A velvet dish! Fie, fie, 'tis lewd and filthy.
Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut shell,
A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap.
Away with it! Come, let me have a bigger.
fullText: Why, this was molded on a porringer! A velvet dish! Fie, fie, 'tis lewd and filthy. Why, 'tis a cockle or a walnut shell, A knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap. Away with it! Come, let me have a bigger.

lines: When you are gentle, you shall have one too,
And not till then.
fullText: When you are gentle, you shall have one too, And not till then.

lines: Why, thou sayst true. It is a paltry cap,
A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie.
I love thee well in that thou lik'st it not.
fullText: Why, thou sayst true. It is a paltry cap, A custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie. I love thee well in that thou lik'st it not.

lines: Thy gown? Why, ay. Come, tailor, let us see 't.
O mercy God, what masking-stuff is here?
What's this? A sleeve? 'Tis like a demi-cannon.
What, up and down carved like an apple tart?
Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash,
Like to a censer in a barber's shop.
Why, what a devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?
fullText: Thy gown? Why, ay. Come, tailor, let us see 't. O mercy God, what masking-stuff is here? What's this? A sleeve? 'Tis like a demi-cannon. What, up and down carved like an apple tart? Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash, Like to a censer in a barber's shop. Why, what a devil's name, tailor, call'st thou this?

lines: Marry, and did. But if you be remembered,
I did not bid you mar it to the time.
Go, hop me over every kennel home,
For you shall hop without my custom, sir.
I'll none of it. Hence, make your best of it.
fullText: Marry, and did. But if you be remembered, I did not bid you mar it to the time. Go, hop me over every kennel home, For you shall hop without my custom, sir. I'll none of it. Hence, make your best of it.

lines: Why, true, he means to make a puppet of thee.
fullText: Why, true, he means to make a puppet of thee.

lines: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread,
thou thimble,
Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail!
Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter cricket, thou!
Braved in mine own house with a skein of thread?
Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant,
Or I shall so be-mete thee with thy yard
As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou liv'st.
I tell thee, I, that thou hast marred her gown.
fullText: O monstrous arrogance! Thou liest, thou thread, thou thimble, Thou yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail! Thou flea, thou nit, thou winter cricket, thou! Braved in mine own house with a skein of thread? Away, thou rag, thou quantity, thou remnant, Or I shall so be-mete thee with thy yard As thou shalt think on prating whilst thou liv'st. I tell thee, I, that thou hast marred her gown.

lines: Read it.
fullText: Read it.

lines: Proceed.
fullText: Proceed.

lines: Ay, there's the villainy.
fullText: Ay, there's the villainy.

lines: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.
fullText: Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.

lines: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.
fullText: Go, take it up unto thy master's use.

lines: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?
fullText: Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?

lines: Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid.
more.
fullText: Hortensio, say thou wilt see the tailor paid. more.

lines: Well, come, my Kate, we will unto your father's,
Even in these honest mean habiliments.
Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor,
For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich,
And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds,
So honor peereth in the meanest habit.
What, is the jay more precious than the lark
Because his feathers are more beautiful?
Or is the adder better than the eel
Because his painted skin contents the eye?
O no, good Kate. Neither art thou the worse
For this poor furniture and mean array.
If thou account'st it shame, lay it on me,
And therefore frolic! We will hence forthwith
To feast and sport us at thy father's house.
him,
And bring our horses unto Long-lane end.
There will we mount, and thither walk on foot.
Let's see, I think 'tis now some seven o'clock,
And well we may come there by dinner time.
fullText: Well, come, my Kate, we will unto your father's, Even in these honest mean habiliments. Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor, For 'tis the mind that makes the body rich, And as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds, So honor peereth in the meanest habit. What, is the jay more precious than the lark Because his feathers are more beautiful? Or is the adder better than the eel Because his painted skin contents the eye? O no, good Kate. Neither art thou the worse For this poor furniture and mean array. If thou account'st it shame, lay it on me, And therefore frolic! We will hence forthwith To feast and sport us at thy father's house. him, And bring our horses unto Long-lane end. There will we mount, and thither walk on foot. Let's see, I think 'tis now some seven o'clock, And well we may come there by dinner time.

lines: It shall be seven ere I go to horse.
Look what I speak, or do, or think to do,
You are still crossing it.--Sirs, let 't alone.
I will not go today, and, ere I do,
It shall be what o'clock I say it is.
fullText: It shall be seven ere I go to horse. Look what I speak, or do, or think to do, You are still crossing it.--Sirs, let 't alone. I will not go today, and, ere I do, It shall be what o'clock I say it is.

lines: Come on, i' God's name, once more toward our
father's.
Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!
fullText: Come on, i' God's name, once more toward our father's. Good Lord, how bright and goodly shines the moon!

lines: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.
fullText: I say it is the moon that shines so bright.

lines: Now, by my mother's son, and that's myself,
It shall be moon, or star, or what I list,
Or e'er I journey to your father's house.
again.--
Evermore crossed and crossed, nothing but crossed!
fullText: Now, by my mother's son, and that's myself, It shall be moon, or star, or what I list, Or e'er I journey to your father's house. again.-- Evermore crossed and crossed, nothing but crossed!

lines: I say it is the moon.
fullText: I say it is the moon.

lines: Nay, then you lie. It is the blessed sun.
fullText: Nay, then you lie. It is the blessed sun.

lines: Well, forward, forward. Thus the bowl should run,
And not unluckily against the bias.
But soft! Company is coming here.
fullText: Well, forward, forward. Thus the bowl should run, And not unluckily against the bias. But soft! Company is coming here.

lines: Why, how now, Kate? I hope thou art not mad!
This is a man--old, wrinkled, faded, withered--
And not a maiden, as thou sayst he is.
fullText: Why, how now, Kate? I hope thou art not mad! This is a man--old, wrinkled, faded, withered-- And not a maiden, as thou sayst he is.

lines: Do, good old grandsire, and withal make known
Which way thou travelest. If along with us,
We shall be joyful of thy company.
fullText: Do, good old grandsire, and withal make known Which way thou travelest. If along with us, We shall be joyful of thy company.

lines: What is his name?
fullText: What is his name?

lines: Happily met, the happier for thy son.
And now by law as well as reverend age,
I may entitle thee my loving father.
The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman,
Thy son by this hath married. Wonder not,
Nor be not grieved. She is of good esteem,
Her dowry wealthy, and of worthy birth;
Beside, so qualified as may beseem
The spouse of any noble gentleman.
Let me embrace with old Vincentio,
And wander we to see thy honest son,
Who will of thy arrival be full joyous.
fullText: Happily met, the happier for thy son. And now by law as well as reverend age, I may entitle thee my loving father. The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman, Thy son by this hath married. Wonder not, Nor be not grieved. She is of good esteem, Her dowry wealthy, and of worthy birth; Beside, so qualified as may beseem The spouse of any noble gentleman. Let me embrace with old Vincentio, And wander we to see thy honest son, Who will of thy arrival be full joyous.

lines: Come, go along and see the truth hereof,
For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.
fullText: Come, go along and see the truth hereof, For our first merriment hath made thee jealous.

lines: Sir, here's the door. This is Lucentio's house.
My father's bears more toward the marketplace.
Thither must I, and here I leave you, sir.
fullText: Sir, here's the door. This is Lucentio's house. My father's bears more toward the marketplace. Thither must I, and here I leave you, sir.

lines: Nay, I told you your son was
well beloved in Padua.--Do you hear, sir? To leave
frivolous circumstances, I pray you tell Signior
Lucentio that his father is come from Pisa and is
here at the door to speak with him.
fullText: Nay, I told you your son was well beloved in Padua.--Do you hear, sir? To leave frivolous circumstances, I pray you tell Signior Lucentio that his father is come from Pisa and is here at the door to speak with him.

lines: Why, how now, gentleman!
Why, this is flat knavery, to take upon you another
man's name.
fullText: Why, how now, gentleman! Why, this is flat knavery, to take upon you another man's name.

lines: Prithee, Kate, let's stand aside and see the
end of this controversy.
fullText: Prithee, Kate, let's stand aside and see the end of this controversy.

lines: First kiss me, Kate, and we will.
fullText: First kiss me, Kate, and we will.

lines: What, art thou ashamed of me?
fullText: What, art thou ashamed of me?

lines: Why, then, let's home again. Come,
sirrah, let's away.
fullText: Why, then, let's home again. Come, sirrah, let's away.

lines: Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate.
Better once than never, for never too late.
fullText: Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate. Better once than never, for never too late.

lines: Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!
fullText: Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!

lines: Padua affords nothing but what is kind.
fullText: Padua affords nothing but what is kind.

lines: Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow!
fullText: Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow!

lines: You are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense:
I mean Hortensio is afeard of you.
fullText: You are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense: I mean Hortensio is afeard of you.

lines: Roundly replied.
fullText: Roundly replied.

lines: Conceives by me? How likes Hortensio that?
fullText: Conceives by me? How likes Hortensio that?

lines: Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.
fullText: Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.

lines: To her, Kate!
fullText: To her, Kate!

lines: A hundred marks, my Kate does put her down.
fullText: A hundred marks, my Kate does put her down.

lines: Spoke like an officer! Ha' to thee, lad.
fullText: Spoke like an officer! Ha' to thee, lad.

lines: Nay, that you shall not. Since you have begun,
Have at you for a bitter jest or two.
fullText: Nay, that you shall not. Since you have begun, Have at you for a bitter jest or two.

lines: She hath prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio,
This bird you aimed at, though you hit her not.--
Therefore a health to all that shot and missed.
fullText: She hath prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio, This bird you aimed at, though you hit her not.-- Therefore a health to all that shot and missed.

lines: A good swift simile, but something currish.
fullText: A good swift simile, but something currish.

lines: He has a little galled me, I confess.
And as the jest did glance away from me,
'Tis ten to one it maimed you two outright.
fullText: He has a little galled me, I confess. And as the jest did glance away from me, 'Tis ten to one it maimed you two outright.

lines: Well, I say no. And therefore, for assurance,
Let's each one send unto his wife,
And he whose wife is most obedient
To come at first when he doth send for her
Shall win the wager which we will propose.
fullText: Well, I say no. And therefore, for assurance, Let's each one send unto his wife, And he whose wife is most obedient To come at first when he doth send for her Shall win the wager which we will propose.

lines: Twenty crowns?
I'll venture so much of my hawk or hound,
But twenty times so much upon my wife.
fullText: Twenty crowns? I'll venture so much of my hawk or hound, But twenty times so much upon my wife.

lines: A match! 'Tis done.
fullText: A match! 'Tis done.

lines: How? "She's busy, and she cannot come"?
Is that an answer?
fullText: How? "She's busy, and she cannot come"? Is that an answer?

lines: I hope better.
fullText: I hope better.

lines: O ho, entreat her!
Nay, then, she must needs come.
fullText: O ho, entreat her! Nay, then, she must needs come.

lines: Worse and worse. She will not come!
O vile, intolerable, not to be endured!--
Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress,
Say I command her come to me.
fullText: Worse and worse. She will not come! O vile, intolerable, not to be endured!-- Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress, Say I command her come to me.

lines: What?
fullText: What?

lines: The fouler fortune mine, and there an end.
fullText: The fouler fortune mine, and there an end.

lines: Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?
fullText: Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?

lines: Go fetch them hither. If they deny to come,
Swinge me them soundly forth unto their husbands.
Away, I say, and bring them hither straight.
fullText: Go fetch them hither. If they deny to come, Swinge me them soundly forth unto their husbands. Away, I say, and bring them hither straight.

lines: Marry, peace it bodes, and love, and quiet life,
An awful rule, and right supremacy,
And, to be short, what not that's sweet and happy.
fullText: Marry, peace it bodes, and love, and quiet life, An awful rule, and right supremacy, And, to be short, what not that's sweet and happy.

lines: Nay, I will win my wager better yet,
And show more sign of her obedience,
Her new-built virtue and obedience.
fullText: Nay, I will win my wager better yet, And show more sign of her obedience, Her new-built virtue and obedience.

lines: Katherine, I charge thee tell these headstrong
women
What duty they do owe their lords and husbands.
fullText: Katherine, I charge thee tell these headstrong women What duty they do owe their lords and husbands.

lines: Come on, I say, and first begin with her.
fullText: Come on, I say, and first begin with her.

lines: I say she shall.--And first begin with her.
fullText: I say she shall.--And first begin with her.

lines: Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.
fullText: Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.

lines: Come, Kate, we'll to bed.
We three are married, but you two are sped.
hit the white,
And being a winner, God give you good night.
fullText: Come, Kate, we'll to bed. We three are married, but you two are sped. hit the white, And being a winner, God give you good night.
